codeReview.summary: Solid implementation of the mercenary companion system across 5 files (2096 lines added). MercenarySystem.ts is well-structured with clear separation of concerns: definitions, hiring/dismissal, stats/leveling, equipment, combat AI per role, damage handling, mana regen, and save/load. ZoneScene integration covers sprite creation, per-frame AI updates, combat execution, tank aggro absorption, death animation, and cleanup. UIScene adds a full companion panel with hire/dismiss/revive UI and camp NPC proximity checks. All 5 mercenary types have Chinese labels and distinct AI roles. 57 unit tests cover definitions, hiring, stats, equipment, death/revival, all 5 AI roles, heal mechanics, save/load roundtrip, and edge cases. TypeScript compiles clean, build succeeds, all 1530 tests pass. Zone transitions correctly preserve mercenary state because MercenarySystem is instantiated in the `isFirstLoad` block and persists across `scene.restart()`. Save/load correctly serializes/deserializes mercenary data as an optional field on SaveData. A few non-blocking issues noted below.

issues.description: canEquipWeapon uses `(item as any)` to access `weaponType` and `type` properties that don't exist on the ItemInstance interface. ItemInstance has only `stats: Partial<Record<string, number>>` — weaponType is on WeaponBase which is a separate interface. This works at runtime because items generated by LootSystem carry these properties, but it's not type-safe. A cleaner approach would be to look up the WeaponBase definition via `item.baseId` or extend ItemInstance to optionally carry `weaponType`.
canEquipArmor also uses `(item as any).type` — same type-safety gap as canEquipWeapon. The `type` field ('weapon'|'armor'|...) exists on ItemBase but not ItemInstance.

Mercenary movement uses a fixed speed of 0.06 tiles/frame rather than being delta-time based. At varying frame rates, the mercenary will move faster or slower. The player movement code at line 1031 has a similar pattern, so this matches existing conventions, but it's worth noting as a future improvement.

When the mercenary attacks and kills a monster (line 3017: onMonsterKilled), the kill is credited fully — including exp, loot, and quest progress. The feature spec says mercenary combat goes through CombatSystem, which it does. However, this means the mercenary can independently trigger kills and quest completions, which may not be an issue but is worth noting for game balance.

handleMercenaryCombat only handles tank aggro (aiRole === 'tank'). Non-tank mercenaries can never be targeted by monsters — all damage goes to the player. This is a valid design choice (tank absorbs, others don't), but it means ranged/healer/mage mercenaries are functionally invulnerable in combat, which may feel imbalanced.

Camp proximity check for hiring/reviving is duplicated between renderHirePanel and renderMercenaryInfo (lines 2001-2012 and 2275-2285). Could be extracted to a helper method to reduce duplication, but functionally correct.

The mock weapon in tests has `weaponType: 'sword'` as a top-level property on the mock object, which matches the runtime `(item as any).weaponType` access pattern but doesn't match the ItemInstance interface. This is consistent with the production code's approach but highlights the type-safety gap.
canEquipWeapon/canEquipArmor use `(item as any)` to access properties (weaponType, type) not on ItemInstance interface — type-unsafe but works at runtime.
Mercenary movement is frame-rate dependent (0.06 tiles/frame) rather than delta-time based. Matches existing player movement pattern.
Only tank mercenaries can be targeted by monsters — other roles are effectively invulnerable. Valid design choice but may feel imbalanced.
Camp proximity check logic is duplicated between renderHirePanel and renderMercenaryInfo. Could be extracted to a shared helper.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker made spawnMercenarySprite() and destroyMercenarySprite() public on ZoneScene so UIScene can call them via `(this.zone as any)`. This matches existing patterns (UIScene accesses ZoneScene internals via `as any` cast), but AGENTS.md doesn't document which ZoneScene methods are part of the 'public API' for UIScene access vs truly private. As more systems add cross-scene methods, this could become confusing.
sharedStateObservations.rationale: Documenting the UIScene ↔ ZoneScene communication pattern (which methods are expected to be called cross-scene) would help future workers maintain clean boundaries.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The ItemInstance type lacks weaponType/type fields that are needed for equipment validation. Multiple systems (MercenarySystem, likely InventorySystem) use `as any` to access these. Adding optional discriminator fields to ItemInstance (e.g., `weaponType?: string`, `itemType?: string`) would eliminate the need for unsafe casts.
sharedStateObservations.rationale: This is a known type-safety gap that affects multiple systems. Documenting it as a convention (or better, fixing the type) would prevent future workers from independently solving it with different approaches.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker's skill feedback suggests checking for pre-existing partial implementations from prior worker sessions. The first worker session (f9655db3) created the MercenarySystem class and tests, and the second session (b699fdde) added the UIScene companion panel and fixed TypeScript issues. The skill doesn't mention checking for pre-existing work.
sharedStateObservations.rationale: Adding a step like 'Check if a prior worker session has already started this feature (look for existing files and tests)' would help workers avoid duplicating effort or conflicting with partial implementations.

summary: The mercenary companion system is a comprehensive, well-implemented feature that fully covers all 11 requirements from the spec. All 5 mercenary types have distinct stats, AI, and Chinese labels. The hiring panel correctly requires camp proximity and gold. Combat AI is role-appropriate (tank intercepts, melee rushes, ranged repositions, healer heals at <60% HP, mage AoEs). Independent leveling with 30% exp share works correctly. Equipment slots modify stats. Death/revival mechanics function properly. Zone transitions preserve state via persistent MercenarySystem instance. Save/load roundtrip is tested. P key toggles the companion panel. 57 tests pass, TypeScript clean, build succeeds. No blocking issues found — only non-blocking type-safety and minor design observations.
